mnemonics.title: Types of Water Pollutants
mnemonics.content: BCPN: Biological (germs), Chemical (toxics), Physical (heat/radiation), Nutritional (excess N-P)

mnemonics.title: Water Treatment Stages
mnemonics.content: PST: Primary (solids removal), Secondary (biological treatment), Tertiary (advanced polishing)

mnemonics.title: Effects of Heavy Metals
mnemonics.content: Lead affects Brain/Nerves, Mercury affects Mind/Movement, Arsenic causes Cancer, Cadmium damages Kidneys

acronyms.usage: Measures organic pollution by oxygen needed for microbial decomposition
Measures total organic matter that can be chemically oxidized
Oxygen available in water for aquatic life; low DO indicates pollution
Measures dissolved salts and minerals indicating water quality
Measures suspended particles affecting water clarity and quality
Facility that treats sewage and industrial effluent before discharge

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bathtub: nutrients (fertilizer runoff) flow in → algae multiply on surface → sunlight blocked → plants below die → bacteria decompose them → oxygen depleted → fish suffocate → 'dead' water remains

Picture a pyramid: bottom has many small fish with small toxin amounts; middle has fewer fish with more toxin; top has few fish (like humans) with concentrated toxins - each level magnifies the pollution
Point source = single pipe discharging (easy to monitor/control); Non-point = rain washing pollution from fields/streets (hard to control, like trying to stop all rain from washing streets)
BOD = biological 'eating' of pollution (slow, measures biodegradable only); COD = chemical 'burning' of pollution (fast, measures everything that can oxidize)

analogies.analogy: Like adding too much spice to soup - a little can enhance, but excess ruins the entire dish. Water can handle some pollution naturally, but beyond its capacity, it becomes harmful.
Like a garden with too much fertilizer - plants initially grow explosively, then crowd out everything, eventually dying and creating a messy, smelly swamp.
Like a slow-moving conveyor belt - once pollutants seep into groundwater, they can travel for decades before appearing somewhere else, making cleanup extremely difficult.
Like plaque on teeth - a slimy layer that harbors bacteria, protects them from cleaning agents, and grows thicker over time if not properly managed.
